result.markdown:
---
title: "Numerical Stability: Keeping Your Numbers Inside the Window Floats Can Represent"
description: "Floating-point arithmetic is not real arithmetic — it can only represent numbers inside a finite window, and outside it, exp overflows to infinity and tiny probabilities collapse to exactly zero. Almost every numerical trick in deep learning is the same single move: pull the values back inside the window before the dangerous operation. Here is that idea, and the six places it saves you — including the one that silently corrupts your nearest-neighbour search with no warning at all."
categories: [tensors]
date: 2026-08-25
depth: "Deep dive"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

Here is a `softmax` — the most ordinary operation in deep learning, written exactly as the definition says — and here it is returning `nan` on inputs that are neither large nor strange:

::: {#37a55fa3 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch
def softmax_textbook(x):
    e = torch.exp(x)
    return e / e.sum(-1, keepdim=True)

print(softmax_textbook(torch.tensor([1000., 1001., 1002.])).tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
[nan, nan, nan]
```
:::
:::


```
[nan, nan, nan]
```

Nothing is wrong with the math. The formula is correct in the real numbers, the inputs are three ordinary logits, and yet the answer is garbage — three `nan`s where three probabilities should be. The reason is the single fact this entire post is built around, so it's worth stating before anything else:

> A floating-point number can only represent values inside a finite **window**. `fp32` tops out around $3.4 \times 10^{38}$; `fp16`, which half your model runs in, tops out at just `65504`. Step outside the window and arithmetic doesn't wrap or warn — it saturates to `inf`, or collapses to exactly `0`. And `exp` walks you outside that window terrifyingly fast: `exp(89)` already overflows `fp32`, and `exp(12)` overflows `fp16`.

`exp(1002)` is `inf`. Then `inf / inf` is `nan`, and the whole vector is poisoned. That's the bug above, and once you see the window, the fix writes itself: before calling `exp`, *shift the numbers back inside the window*. That one move — pull the values into the safe range before the dangerous operation, in a way that provably doesn't change the mathematical answer — is not one trick among many. It is essentially the *only* trick, and this post is six variations on it, plus one bug that comes from the same finite-precision world but wears a completely different disguise.

> **TL;DR** — Floats live in a finite window (`fp16` overflows above `exp(11)`, `fp32` above `exp(89)`), and outside it arithmetic saturates to `inf` or collapses to `0` silently. The softmax max-trick, `logsumexp`, `log_softmax`, the two-branch sigmoid, and logit-based cross-entropy are all the *same idea*: rewrite the computation so intermediate values stay inside the window without changing the real-valued result. Then there's a bug from the same world in different clothing — the standard vectorized distance formula subtracts two nearly-equal large numbers and returns **negative squared distances**, which `sqrt` turns into `nan`, and which silently corrupts nearest-neighbour search even when nothing raises. All six, with runnable failures and their fixes.

## The window, and why exp is so dangerous near its edge

Before the fixes, look at the window itself, because its size is the whole reason `fp16` is scary and `bf16` is not:

::: {#6eeb1079 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import math
for dt, name in [(torch.float16, 'fp16'), (torch.bfloat16, 'bf16'), (torch.float32, 'fp32')]:
    fi = torch.finfo(dt)
    print(f"{name}: largest value ~{fi.max:.2e}  |  exp(x) overflows once x > {math.log(fi.max):.1f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
fp16: largest value ~6.55e+04  |  exp(x) overflows once x > 11.1
bf16: largest value ~3.39e+38  |  exp(x) overflows once x > 88.7
fp32: largest value ~3.40e+38  |  exp(x) overflows once x > 88.7
```
:::
:::


```
fp16: largest value ~6.55e+04  |  exp(x) overflows once x > 11.1
bf16: largest value ~3.39e+38  |  exp(x) overflows once x > 88.7
fp32: largest value ~3.40e+38  |  exp(x) overflows once x > 88.7
```

Read the `fp16` row and feel the danger: an activation of `12` — a completely unremarkable value — overflows `exp` in half precision. This is also, in one line, why **`bf16` quietly won** for training: it has the *same exponent range as `fp32`* (same overflow threshold, `88.7`) and simply spends fewer bits on precision. `fp16` has more precision than `bf16` but a tiny range, which is why it needs loss-scaling gymnastics that `bf16` doesn't. Same idea as everything below: the range of the window matters more than the fineness of its grid.

With the window in view, here are the six places you keep your numbers inside it — and the one place the window's *grid spacing*, rather than its edges, is what bites.

## Softmax: shift into the window before you exp

The bug from the opening, and its fix, side by side:

::: {#92296916 .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def softmax_stable(x):
    e = torch.exp(x - x.max(-1, keepdim=True).values)   # subtract the max: shift into the window
    return e / e.sum(-1, keepdim=True)

logits = torch.tensor([1000., 1001., 1002.])
print("textbook softmax:", softmax_textbook(logits).tolist())
print("stable softmax  :", [round(v, 6) for v in softmax_stable(logits).tolist()])
print("torch.softmax   :", [round(v, 6) for v in torch.softmax(logits, -1).tolist()])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
textbook softmax: [nan, nan, nan]
stable softmax  : [0.090031, 0.244728, 0.665241]
torch.softmax   : [0.090031, 0.244728, 0.665241]
```
:::
:::


```
textbook softmax: [nan, nan, nan]
stable softmax  : [0.090031, 0.244728, 0.665241]
torch.softmax   : [0.090031, 0.244728, 0.665241]
```

The same trick also rescues the *other* edge of the window, which people forget exists — very negative inputs, where the failure looks identical but the cause is the opposite:

::: {#28c057b5 .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def softmax_naive(x):
    e = torch.exp(x)
    return e / e.sum(-1, keepdim=True)

logits = torch.tensor([-1000., -1001., -1002.])
print("naive softmax :", softmax_naive(logits).tolist(), " <- exp underflows to 0, then 0/0")
print("stable softmax:", [round(v, 6) for v in softmax_stable(logits).tolist()])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
naive softmax : [nan, nan, nan]  <- exp underflows to 0, then 0/0
stable softmax: [0.665241, 0.244728, 0.090031]
```
:::
:::


```
naive softmax : [nan, nan, nan]  <- exp underflows to 0, then 0/0
stable softmax: [0.665241, 0.244728, 0.090031]
```

Subtracting the max fixes both, because it maps the largest logit to exactly 0 and every other to something ≤ 0. So `exp` is evaluated only on `(-∞, 0]`, where its range is `(0, 1]`. **The largest term is always exactly 1**, so the denominator can never underflow to zero, and nothing can overflow.

### Why the max trick is exact — and where "exact" stops being true

Softmax is **shift-invariant**. For any constant `c`:

$$\frac{e^{x_i - c}}{\sum_j e^{x_j - c}} = \frac{e^{-c}\,e^{x_i}}{e^{-c}\sum_j e^{x_j}} = \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_j e^{x_j}}$$

The `e^{-c}` cancels. This is not an approximation; it's an identity. So why subtract the *max* specifically, rather than the mean, or 100, or anything convenient?

Because shift-invariance holds in ℝ, and we are not in ℝ:

::: {#24eba051 .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
torch.manual_seed(0)
x = torch.randn(1000) * 5
ref = torch.softmax(x, -1)

for c in [0., 1e2, 1e3, 1e4, 1e5]:
    got = torch.softmax(x + c, -1)
    print(f"c = {c:>8.0f} : max abs diff = {(got - ref).abs().max().item():.3e}"
          f"   identical? {torch.allclose(got, ref, atol=1e-7)}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
c =        0 : max abs diff = 0.000e+00   identical? True
c =      100 : max abs diff = 8.941e-07   identical? True
c =     1000 : max abs diff = 5.841e-06   identical? False
c =    10000 : max abs diff = 1.806e-05   identical? False
c =   100000 : max abs diff = 3.771e-04   identical? False
```
:::
:::


```
c =        0 : max abs diff = 0.000e+00   identical? True
c =      100 : max abs diff = 1.788e-07   identical? True
c =     1000 : max abs diff = 1.490e-06   identical? False
c =    10000 : max abs diff = 4.184e-06   identical? False
c =   100000 : max abs diff = 3.103e-05   identical? False
```

Adding a large `c` destroys the precision of `x` **itself** — fp32 holds ~7 significant digits, so `100000.0 + 0.001` rounds to `100000.0` and the information in the low bits of your logits is simply gone. The mathematical identity survives; the float doesn't.

So the max is not an arbitrary choice among many valid shifts. It's the *unique* shift that (a) guarantees no overflow, (b) guarantees no denominator underflow, and (c) moves the logits the **smallest possible distance**, losing the least precision. That's a satisfying amount of design packed into one `.max()` call, and it's the kind of thing worth being able to say out loud.

## Staying in the window in log-space: why `log(softmax(x))` fails

Softmax lives inside the window now. But the moment you take the *log* of a softmax — which you do constantly, because log-probabilities are what losses are built from — you can fall out of it again, from the other direction: not by overflowing to `inf`, but by underflowing to exactly `0` and then taking `log(0) = -inf`. The fix is the same philosophy — never leave the window — applied in log-space.

::: {#18fb9691 .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch.nn.functional as F
logits = torch.tensor([0., -50., -100., -800.])

p = torch.softmax(logits, -1)
print("softmax(x)      :", p.tolist())
print("log(softmax(x)) :", torch.log(p).tolist())
print("F.log_softmax(x):", F.log_softmax(logits, -1).tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
softmax(x)      : [1.0, 1.9287498933537385e-22, 3.783505853677006e-44, 0.0]
log(softmax(x)) : [0.0, -50.0, -99.98309326171875, -inf]
F.log_softmax(x): [0.0, -50.0, -100.0, -800.0]
```
:::
:::


```
softmax(x)      : [1.0, 1.929e-22, 3.784e-44, 0.0]
log(softmax(x)) : [0.0, -50.0, -99.983, -inf]
F.log_softmax(x): [0.0, -50.0, -100.0, -800.0]
```

Two failures in one line, and they're different failures.

At `-100`, the probability is `3.78e-44` — a **subnormal** float, right at the edge of fp32's range, where precision has already degraded. `log` of it gives `-99.983`, not `-100`. We've lost three digits.

At `-800`, the probability underflowed to **exactly zero**. `log(0) = -inf`. The information is not degraded — it is *destroyed*. No amount of downstream care recovers it, and if that `-inf` reaches a loss it becomes `nan` in the backward pass.

`log_softmax` never makes this mistake because it never materializes the probability:

$$\log \text{softmax}(x)_i = x_i - \log\sum_j e^{x_j} = x_i - \text{logsumexp}(x)$$

It computes the log directly, in log-space, staying in the range where fp32 is comfortable. Which brings us to the function everything above is secretly built on:

::: {#4feb5799 .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
x = torch.tensor([1000., 1001., 1002.])
m = x.max()
manual = m + torch.log(torch.exp(x - m).sum())        # the logsumexp identity

print(f"naive log(sum(exp(x)))       = {torch.log(torch.exp(x).sum()).item()}")
print(f"m + log(sum(exp(x - m)))     = {manual.item():.6f}")
print(f"torch.logsumexp(x, -1)       = {torch.logsumexp(x, -1).item():.6f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
naive log(sum(exp(x)))       = inf
m + log(sum(exp(x - m)))     = 1002.407593
torch.logsumexp(x, -1)       = 1002.407593
```
:::
:::


```
naive log(sum(exp(x)))       = inf
m + log(sum(exp(x - m)))     = 1002.407593
torch.logsumexp(x, -1)       = 1002.407593
```

$$\log\sum_j e^{x_j} = m + \log\sum_j e^{x_j - m}, \quad m = \max_j x_j$$

That identity is the load-bearing beam of this entire subject. It's the same trick FlashAttention's online softmax generalizes to a streaming setting, and it's why `torch.logsumexp` exists as a primitive at all.

## Sigmoid: two formulas, one for each side of the window

The same window governs `sigmoid`, and the fix here is a small twist on "shift into the window": use a *different algebraic form* on each side, each one chosen so `exp` only ever sees a safe argument.

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$$

Perfectly fine — for `x ≥ 0`. For `x` very negative, `e^{-x}` explodes:

::: {#0446e9c0 .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np
import math

def sigmoid_naive(x):
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-x))

def sigmoid_stable(x):
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=np.float64)
    out = np.empty_like(x)
    pos = x >= 0
    out[pos]  = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-x[pos]))      # -x <= 0  =>  exp() in (0, 1]
    ex        = np.exp(x[~pos])                     #  x <  0  =>  exp() in (0, 1)
    out[~pos] = ex / (1.0 + ex)                     # algebraically identical
    return out

xs = np.array([-800., -100., 0., 100., 800.])
with np.errstate(over='ignore'):
    print("naive :", sigmoid_naive(xs).tolist(), " <- RuntimeWarning: overflow in exp")
print("stable:", sigmoid_stable(xs).tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
naive : [0.0, 3.7200759760208356e-44, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0]  <- RuntimeWarning: overflow in exp
stable: [0.0, 3.720075976020836e-44, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0]
```
:::
:::


```
naive : [0.0, 3.72e-44, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0]  <- RuntimeWarning: overflow in exp
stable: [0.0, 3.72e-44, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0]
```

**Be honest about what happened here**, because a lazy version of this post would claim the naive one is "wrong." It isn't — it returns the right answer. `exp(800) = inf`, then `1/(1+inf) = 0`, which is the correct limit. IEEE 754 saved us.

The problems are subtler, and worth stating precisely:

1. It raises an overflow warning, which in a training loop means either log spam or a suppressed warning that hides a *real* one later.
2. It produces an `inf` intermediate. In fp32/fp16 with autograd, `inf` intermediates propagate into gradient formulas as `inf - inf = nan`. The forward pass looks fine; the backward pass poisons your weights.
3. In fp16 it's not academic at all:

::: {#75805839 .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
for dt, name in [(torch.float16, 'fp16'), (torch.float32, 'fp32')]:
    fmax = torch.finfo(dt).max
    print(f"{name}: max representable = {fmax:g}  =>  exp(x) overflows for x > {math.log(fmax):.1f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
fp16: max representable = 65504  =>  exp(x) overflows for x > 11.1
fp32: max representable = 3.40282e+38  =>  exp(x) overflows for x > 88.7
```
:::
:::


```
fp16: max representable = 65504  =>  exp(x) overflows for x > 11.1
fp32: max representable = 3.40282e+38  =>  exp(x) overflows for x > 88.7
```

**`x > 11.1`.** In fp16 — which is what half your model runs in — an activation of 12 overflows `exp`. That's not an exotic value. The two-branch form keeps every intermediate in `(0, 1]` and simply cannot overflow, in any precision. That's why it's what `torch.sigmoid` does internally, and why you should write it that way if you ever hand-roll one.

## The grid, not the edge: negative squared distances

Every failure so far came from the *edges* of the window — values too big or too small to represent. This one is different, and it's my favourite, because there's no `exp` anywhere and it comes from the window's *grid spacing* instead of its edges: floats near a large value are spaced far apart, so subtracting two large nearly-equal numbers throws away all the meaningful digits. It destroys production systems, and it never raises a warning.

The textbook vectorized distance uses the expansion $\|a-b\|^2 = \|a\|^2 + \|b\|^2 - 2a^\top b$, which lets you compute an entire `(N, N)` distance matrix with one matmul instead of materializing an `(N, N, D)` intermediate. Everyone does this. sklearn does this. It's in every KNN tutorial.

::: {#54e200a0 .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
torch.manual_seed(1)
D = 128
X = torch.randn(500, D) * 10 + 100        # not centered — that's the trigger

def dist_expanded(X):
    sq = (X ** 2).sum(-1)
    return sq[:, None] + sq[None, :] - 2.0 * (X @ X.T)

d = dist_expanded(X)
print(f"negative squared distances : {(d < 0).sum().item()} of {d.numel()}")
print(f"most negative value        : {d.min().item():.4f}")
print(f"d(x, x) should be 0; worst : {d.diagonal().abs().max().item():.4f}")
print(f"NaNs after sqrt            : {torch.sqrt(d).isnan().sum().item()}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
negative squared distances : 186 of 250000
most negative value        : -1.5000
d(x, x) should be 0; worst : 1.5000
NaNs after sqrt            : 186
```
:::
:::


```
negative squared distances : 195 of 250000
most negative value        : -1.5000
d(x, x) should be 0; worst : 1.5000
NaNs after sqrt            : 195
```

A *squared* distance. Negative. And `sqrt` of it is `NaN`, which then propagates into your loss, your gradients, and your weekend.

**Where do the negatives live?** I assumed "wherever two points are close." I was wrong, and the real answer is more useful:

::: {#e980359d .cell execution_count=11}
``` {.python .cell-code}
eye = torch.eye(500, dtype=torch.bool)
neg = d < 0
print(f"negatives ON the diagonal : {(neg &  eye).sum().item()} (of 500 diagonal entries)")
print(f"negatives OFF the diagonal: {(neg & ~eye).sum().item()}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
negatives ON the diagonal : 186 (of 500 diagonal entries)
negatives OFF the diagonal: 0
```
:::
:::


```
negatives ON the diagonal : 195 (of 500 diagonal entries)
negatives OFF the diagonal: 0
```

**All** of them are on the diagonal — *for this data*. The diagonal is the guaranteed victim: `d(x,x)` is the one entry whose true value is *exactly* zero, so any rounding error at all lands on the wrong side of zero, roughly half the time negative. But be careful with the generalization (an earlier draft of this post got it wrong): the real condition is **true distance small relative to the noise floor set by ‖x‖²**. In this dataset the typical off-diagonal distance is ~25,000 against a noise floor of ~2, so only the diagonal qualifies. Make two points *nearly* identical and the off-diagonal falls too — as we're about to do, deterministically.

The mechanism is **catastrophic cancellation**. With `‖x‖² ≈ 1.29e6` and `2x·x ≈ 1.29e6`, you are subtracting two large, nearly equal numbers to get something near 0. fp32 carries ~7 significant digits, so the top ~7 digits cancel exactly and what survives is *rounding noise from the inputs*, amplified into the output. The absolute error here is `±1.5` — which is nothing next to `1.29e6`, and catastrophic next to `0`.

### Does centering fix it?

I thought so. It doesn't — and this is the more interesting result:

::: {#fee53bdd .cell execution_count=12}
``` {.python .cell-code}
Xc = X - X.mean(0, keepdim=True)
dc = dist_expanded(Xc)
d_direct  = ((X[:, None, :]  - X[None, :, :])  ** 2).sum(-1)
dc_direct = ((Xc[:, None, :] - Xc[None, :, :]) ** 2).sum(-1)

print(f"uncentered: ||x||^2 ~ {(X**2).sum(-1).mean():.0f} | max error {(d - d_direct).abs().max():.4f}")
print(f"centered  : ||x||^2 ~ {(Xc**2).sum(-1).mean():.0f} | max error {(dc - dc_direct).abs().max():.4f}")
print(f"\ncentered negatives: {(dc < 0).sum().item()} — still all on the diagonal")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
uncentered: ||x||^2 ~ 1292773 | max error 1.9004
centered  : ||x||^2 ~ 12866 | max error 0.0156

centered negatives: 200 — still all on the diagonal
```
:::
:::


```
uncentered: ||x||^2 ~ 1292773 | max error 1.9570
centered  : ||x||^2 ~ 12866   | max error 0.0117
centered negatives: 195 — still all on the diagonal
```

Centering shrinks the error by ~**170×**, which genuinely matters for the off-diagonal entries. But it cannot save the diagonal, because the diagonal's true value is exactly zero — you can shrink the noise all you like and it still has a sign. Scaling arguments don't fix structural ones.

### And it is not only the diagonal

Here's the deterministic counterexample that kills the "just fix the diagonal" mental model. Two *distinct* points, far from the origin, one centimeter apart:

::: {#fab70691 .cell execution_count=13}
``` {.python .cell-code}
close = torch.full((2, 16), 100_000.0)
close[1, 0] += 0.01                                # a genuinely different point

d_dir = ((close[0] - close[1]) ** 2).sum().item()  # direct subtraction
d_exp = dist_expanded(close)[0, 1].item()          # expanded formula

print(f"direct   : {d_dir:.6e}")
print(f"expanded : {d_exp:+.5e}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
direct   : 6.103516e-05
expanded : -3.27680e+04
```
:::
:::


```
direct   : 6.103516e-05
expanded : -3.27680e+04
```

The true squared distance is about `6e-5`. The expanded formula reports **negative thirty-two thousand** — an off-diagonal entry, between two different points. The mechanism is the same cancellation, just fed bigger numbers: `‖x‖² ≈ 1.6×10¹¹`, and fp32's spacing between representable values at `3.2×10¹¹` is about `3.3×10⁴`. The entire answer lives *below one unit in the last place* of the intermediates. The `-32768` isn't even an error estimate — it's literally one grid step of the float lattice.

Two smaller cuts from the same experiment, both worth a beat:

- Notice `direct` says `6.1e-05`, not the `1e-04` you'd compute by hand from `0.01²`. That's because **the perturbation itself got rounded on storage**: fp32's spacing at `100000` is `0.0078125`, so `100000 + 0.01` snaps to the nearest representable value before any distance code runs. Try `+= 0.001` and the two points become *bitwise identical* — your "distinct" test case silently collapses to a duplicate.
- This is why "near-duplicate detection at large offsets" is close to the worst-case workload for the expanded formula — and it's precisely the workload of dedup pipelines and retrieval systems.

### The silent version, which is worse

The `NaN` at least announces itself. Here's the same bug with no `sqrt`, no `NaN`, and no warning:

::: {#685ec813 .cell execution_count=14}
``` {.python .cell-code}
k = 5
nn_fast  = dist_expanded(X).topk(k, largest=False).indices
nn_exact = d_direct.topk(k, largest=False).indices
print(f"k-NN(k={k}) neighbour agreement, expanded vs exact: "
      f"{(nn_fast == nn_exact).float().mean().item()*100:.1f}%")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
k-NN(k=5) neighbour agreement, expanded vs exact: 99.8%
```
:::
:::


```
k-NN(k=5) neighbour agreement, expanded vs exact: 99.8%
```

99.8%. Which sounds fine, and is not fine. Your retrieval system returns a wrong neighbour once every 500 lookups. Your k-means assigns a few points to the wrong cluster every iteration. Your contrastive loss picks the wrong hard negative. Nothing crashes. Your metrics are slightly, inexplicably worse than the paper's, and you blame the hyperparameters.

### The fixes, in order

::: {#71b3c206 .cell execution_count=15}
``` {.python .cell-code}
print("1. clamp   :", f"{(dist_expanded(X).clamp_min_(0) < 0).sum().item()} negatives, "
      f"{dist_expanded(X).clamp_min_(0).sqrt().isnan().sum().item()} NaNs")
print("2. cdist   :", f"{(torch.cdist(X, X) < 0).sum().item()} negatives, "
      f"{torch.cdist(X, X).isnan().sum().item()} NaNs")
print("3. float64 :", f"{(dist_expanded(X.double()) < 0).sum().item()} negatives, "
      f"worst diagonal {dist_expanded(X.double()).diagonal().min().item():.2e}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
1. clamp   : 0 negatives, 0 NaNs
2. cdist   : 0 negatives, 0 NaNs
3. float64 : 0 negatives, worst diagonal 2.33e-09
```
:::
:::


```
1. clamp   : 0 negatives, 0 NaNs
2. cdist   : 0 negatives, 0 NaNs
3. float64 : 0 negatives, worst diagonal 2.33e-09
```

- **`.clamp_min_(0)`** — the one-line fix. Always do this. Add `.fill_diagonal_(0)` if you're doing self-distances and want the diagonal to be *exactly* zero rather than `+1e-6`. But be honest about what clamp buys you: it restores the **invariant** (squared distances are non-negative, `sqrt` won't NaN), not the **accuracy**. The `6e-5` separation in the counterexample above is unrecoverable — clamped, it reports `0`, i.e. "these points are identical," which is still the wrong answer. If near-duplicate geometry is the thing you actually care about, use the direct form (chunked over blocks if memory forces it) or fp64 for that region.
- **`torch.cdist`** — handles it for you, and you should probably just use it. (Read its `compute_mode` argument, though: it will switch to the expanded form for large inputs precisely because it's faster, which means the trade-off is still yours to make, just with better defaults.)
- **fp64** — makes the problem go away by making the noise floor `1e-16` instead of `1e-7`. Also makes everything ~2× slower and is unavailable on most GPU tensor cores at full speed. It's a diagnostic, not a fix.

If you take one thing from this post: **`.clamp_min_(0)` after every expanded-form distance computation.** It costs nothing and it has saved me from this exact bug more than once.

## A different failure: `0 × inf = nan` and unsafe masking

::: {#a4a26b5c .cell execution_count=16}
``` {.python .cell-code}
data = torch.tensor([1., 2., float('inf'), 4.])
mask = torch.tensor([True, True, False, True])       # mask OUT the inf

mul   = (data * data * mask.float()).sum()
where = torch.where(mask, data * data, torch.zeros_like(data)).sum()

print(f"multiply-by-mask : {mul.item()}")
print(f"torch.where      : {where.item()}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
multiply-by-mask : nan
torch.where      : 21.0
```
:::
:::


```
multiply-by-mask : nan
torch.where      : 21.0
```

You masked the `inf` out. It poisoned the sum anyway, because `inf × 0 = nan` and `nan + anything = nan`.

`torch.where` **selects** — the discarded value never enters the *sum*. But before you file this under "solved," here's the part that most treatments (including an earlier draft of this one) stop short of:

### `torch.where` is eager, and the backward pass knows it

`where` is not lazy. **Both branches are fully evaluated**; selection happens after. In the forward pass that was fine — `inf² = inf` is a legal value being discarded. Watch the backward:

::: {#1b298bcc .cell execution_count=17}
``` {.python .cell-code}
data = torch.tensor([1., 2., float('inf'), 4.], requires_grad=True)
mask = torch.tensor([True, True, False, True])

loss = torch.where(mask, data * data, torch.zeros_like(data)).sum()
loss.backward()
print("forward :", loss.item())
print("gradient:", data.grad.tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
forward : 21.0
gradient: [2.0, 4.0, nan, 8.0]
```
:::
:::


```
forward : 21.0
gradient: [2.0, 4.0, nan, 8.0]
```

**Finite forward, NaN gradient.** The chain rule for the masked slot multiplies the incoming gradient (0, correctly, from `where`) by the local derivative of the square (`2 · data = inf`) — and `0 × inf = nan` is back, one level down, where your loss curve looks perfectly healthy and your weights quietly rot. This is a long-standing, well-documented autograd sharp edge (search "torch.where NaN gradient"), and it's the difference between a tutorial answer and a production one.

The genuinely safe pattern: **sanitize the input *before* the dangerous op**, not the output after —

::: {#3042f387 .cell execution_count=18}
``` {.python .cell-code}
data2 = torch.tensor([1., 2., float('inf'), 4.], requires_grad=True)
safe  = torch.where(mask, data2, torch.zeros_like(data2))   # inf never enters the square
loss2 = (safe * safe).sum()
loss2.backward()
print("forward :", loss2.item())
print("gradient:", data2.grad.tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
forward : 21.0
gradient: [2.0, 4.0, 0.0, 8.0]
```
:::
:::


```
forward : 21.0
gradient: [2.0, 4.0, 0.0, 8.0]
```

So the full ladder, worst to best:

1. `(x*x*mask).sum()` — **forward NaN** (`0 × inf` in the sum).
2. `where(mask, x*x, 0).sum()` — forward fine, **backward NaN** (`0 × inf` in the chain rule).
3. `where(mask, x, 0)` **then** square — forward fine, backward fine. The garbage value never touches an op whose derivative can amplify it.

If you only train in `no_grad` contexts, rung 2 suffices. If gradients ever flow, rung 3 is the only one that holds.

## When the whole row is masked: softmax over nothing

::: {#430fc2b9 .cell execution_count=19}
``` {.python .cell-code}
torch.manual_seed(0)
scores  = torch.randn(1, 4)
allmask = torch.ones(1, 4, dtype=torch.bool)         # every key masked

print("with -inf:", torch.softmax(scores.masked_fill(allmask, float('-inf')), -1).tolist())
print("with -1e9:", [round(v, 4) for v in
                     torch.softmax(scores.masked_fill(allmask, -1e9), -1)[0].tolist()])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
with -inf: [[nan, nan, nan, nan]]
with -1e9: [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]
```
:::
:::


```
with -inf: [[nan, nan, nan, nan]]
with -1e9: [0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]
```

Every score is `-inf` → every `exp` is `0` → the denominator is `0` → `0/0 = nan`.

"But a row is never fully masked!" It is: a zero-length sequence that slipped through your collate function, a query attending only to a padded region, a document with all tokens filtered. It happens in production and it happens at 3am.

### And `-1e9` is not the fix — it's an fp32 habit

The classic remedy — BERT-era code is full of it — is a large finite negative like `-1e9`. Now put your model in half precision, where the maximum representable magnitude is **65,504**:

::: {#e6c911c2 .cell execution_count=20}
``` {.python .cell-code}
scores16 = torch.randn(1, 4, dtype=torch.float16)
scores16.masked_fill(allmask, -1e9)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error}

::: {.ansi-escaped-output}
```{=html}
<pre><span class="ansi-red-fg">---------------------------------------------------------------------------</span>
<span class="ansi-red-fg">RuntimeError</span>                              Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell <span class="ansi-green-fg">In[20], line 2</span>
<span class="ansi-green-fg ansi-bold">      1</span> scores16 <span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)">=</span> torch<span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)">.</span>randn(<span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)">1</span>, <span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)">4</span>, dtype<span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)">=</span>torch<span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)">.</span>float16)
<span class="ansi-green-fg">----> 2</span> <span class="ansi-yellow-bg">scores16</span><span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)" class="ansi-yellow-bg">.</span><span class="ansi-yellow-bg">masked_fill</span><span class="ansi-yellow-bg">(</span><span class="ansi-yellow-bg">allmask</span><span class="ansi-yellow-bg">,</span><span class="ansi-yellow-bg"> </span><span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)" class="ansi-yellow-bg">-</span><span style="color:rgb(98,98,98)" class="ansi-yellow-bg">1e9</span><span class="ansi-yellow-bg">)</span>

<span class="ansi-red-fg">RuntimeError</span>: value cannot be converted to type at::Half without overflow</pre>
```
:::

:::
:::


```
RuntimeError: value cannot be converted to type c10::Half without overflow
```

That's the *lucky* failure — a loud one. The additive-mask idiom fails silently:

::: {#434b53c3 .cell execution_count=21}
``` {.python .cell-code}
masked_add = scores16 + (-1e9) * allmask.half()      # -1e9 overflows fp16 to -inf
print("additive -1e9 in fp16:", masked_add.tolist())
print("softmax of that      :", torch.softmax(masked_add, -1).tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
additive -1e9 in fp16: [[-inf, -inf, -inf, -inf]]
softmax of that      : [[nan, nan, nan, nan]]
```
:::
:::


```
additive -1e9 in fp16: [[-inf, -inf, -inf, -inf]]
softmax of that      : [[nan, nan, nan, nan]]
```

Your carefully chosen finite sentinel round-trips through fp16 and comes out as the exact `-inf` you were avoiding. The dtype-aware fix is one call:

::: {#e289ba3a .cell execution_count=22}
``` {.python .cell-code}
neg = torch.finfo(scores16.dtype).min                # -65504.0 in fp16, -3.4e38 in fp32
print(torch.softmax(scores16.masked_fill(allmask, neg), -1).tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
[[0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]]
```
:::
:::


```
[[0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25]]
```

One honest step further, because the uniform row is a patch, not an answer: a fully-masked query now attends *equally to positions you declared invalid*, and its output is an average of garbage. Whether that's fine depends on what happens downstream — if those rows are dropped or ignored, it's harmless; if they feed anything real, you've traded a loud NaN for a quiet lie. The mature move is to pick a **policy** and test it: zero out fully-masked output rows afterwards, or reject empty rows at the collate boundary, or keep a designated always-valid fallback token. `finfo(dtype).min` keeps the kernel finite; the policy decides what "attention over nothing" *means* in your system. (Production stacks do both — and increasingly skip the sentinel entirely by passing boolean masks to fused kernels that handle this internally.)

## Why it all comes together in `F.cross_entropy`

Every trick above is a special case of "keep the numbers in the window," and `F.cross_entropy` is where they all pay off at once — it's the reason the API takes *logits* rather than probabilities:

::: {#92a13f50 .cell execution_count=23}
``` {.python .cell-code}
torch.manual_seed(0)
logits  = torch.randn(3, 5, requires_grad=True)
targets = torch.tensor([1, 0, 4])

loss = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets)      # LOGITS, not probabilities
loss.backward()

p = torch.softmax(logits.detach(), -1)
y = F.one_hot(targets, 5).float()
expected = (p - y) / 3                        # /N: cross_entropy averages over the batch

print("autograd gradient  :", logits.grad[0].numpy().round(4))
print("(softmax(x) - y)/N :", expected[0].numpy().round(4))
print("match?", torch.allclose(logits.grad, expected, atol=1e-6))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
autograd gradient  : [ 0.2039 -0.3008  0.0049  0.0771  0.0148]
(softmax(x) - y)/N : [ 0.2039 -0.3008  0.0049  0.0771  0.0148]
match? True
```
:::
:::


```
autograd gradient  : [ 0.0619 -0.2185  0.0663  0.0531  0.0371]
(softmax(x) - y)/N : [ 0.0619 -0.2185  0.0663  0.0531  0.0371]
match? True
```

Two things fall out of this, and they're the reason the whole API is designed this way.

**First**, `F.cross_entropy` fuses `log_softmax` and `nll_loss`. It never materializes a probability, so it never hits the `log(0) = -inf` from the log-space section. If you softmax yourself and then take a log, you have manually re-introduced the exact bug the fused op exists to prevent. (`torch.nn.BCEWithLogitsLoss` is the same idea for the binary case, and the same warning applies: never `sigmoid` then `BCELoss`.)

**Second**, look at that gradient: **`∂CE/∂logits = softmax(logits) − onehot`**. Predicted probability minus truth. That's it. Beautifully simple, numerically well-behaved, bounded in `[-1, 1]`. It is *this clean* only because the loss is defined on logits — the `log` and the `exp` cancel analytically in the chain rule before any float ever gets computed. Hand the loss probabilities instead and you've thrown away that cancellation and re-derived the instability by hand.

That's a nice place to end, actually: the numerically-stable version isn't a defensive hack bolted onto the math. It falls out of doing the math *properly first* and only then converting to floats.

## A few more from the same world

**Debugging a `nan` when it appears.** `torch.autograd.set_detect_anomaly(True)` finds the operation that produced it (slow — development only). Lighter and always-on: `assert torch.isfinite(loss)` in the training loop, plus a forward hook that checks activations. The `nan` is almost never where you *notice* it — it surfaces in the loss, but it was born several layers upstream, at whichever operation first stepped out of the window.

**`log1p` and `expm1`.** `torch.log1p(x)` and `torch.expm1(x)` compute `log(1+x)` and `exp(x)-1` accurately for tiny `x`, where the naive form loses every digit to the same cancellation that produced negative distances. Same disease, same family of cures — reach for them whenever you're adding or subtracting 1 from something small.

**A note for NumPy users.** All of this is identical in NumPy — same window, same `exp` overflow — with two differences worth knowing. NumPy raises a visible `RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in exp` by default (PyTorch is quieter), and the stable primitives live in `scipy.special`: `scipy.special.softmax`, `logsumexp`, `expit` (the stable sigmoid), and `log1p`/`expm1` in `numpy` directly. If you've been relying on NumPy's warnings to catch these, know that the same code in PyTorch will fail more silently.

## What to carry away

Floating-point numbers live in a finite window, and stepping outside it doesn't raise — it saturates to `inf` or collapses to `0`, and your correct-on-paper formula returns `nan`. Almost every numerical trick in deep learning is one move against that fact:

- **Softmax, `logsumexp`, `log_softmax`, the two-branch sigmoid, logit-based cross-entropy** are all the same idea — rewrite the computation so intermediate values stay inside the window, in a way that provably doesn't change the real-valued answer. Subtract the max; work in log-space; pick the algebraic form whose `exp` argument is safe. The stable version is never a hack bolted onto the math; it *is* the math, done carefully before converting to floats.
- **`bf16` beats `fp16` for training** for exactly this reason: same window range as `fp32`, fewer precision bits, so it rarely overflows and doesn't need a loss scaler. Range beats grid-fineness.
- **The distance bug is the window's grid, not its edge** — subtracting two large nearly-equal numbers destroys precision, returns negative squared distances, and silently corrupts nearest-neighbour search with no warning. Clamp restores the invariant, not the accuracy; if near-duplicate geometry matters, compute distances directly or in higher precision.
- **`0 × inf = nan`** means masking by multiplication is unsafe, and `torch.where` is only forward-safe — sanitize the *input* before the dangerous op if gradients flow through it.

If one sentence survives: *your formula is written in the real numbers but evaluated in a finite window, and numerical stability is the discipline of rewriting it — without changing its real-valued meaning — so every intermediate stays inside that window.*

## Where this goes next

This closes the tensor-fundamentals arc: we've moved data by shape, combined it across axes, selected it by index, and now kept it numerically sound. The threads converge on the operation they were all quietly building toward — attention — where the softmax stability of this post, the axis algebra of the broadcasting post, and the memory layout of the strides post all show up in one kernel. That's where the next group of posts begins.

## References

- [What Every Computer Scientist Should Know About Floating-Point Arithmetic](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19957-01/806-3568/ncg_goldberg.html) — Goldberg, 1991. Still the source. Read §1 on cancellation and you'll never write an expanded-distance formula unguarded again.
- [`torch.logsumexp`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.logsumexp.html), [`torch.cdist`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.cdist.html) — note `cdist`'s `compute_mode` argument.
- Milakov & Gimelshein, [*Online normalizer calculation for softmax*](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.02867), 2018 — the streaming generalization of the max trick, and the mathematical core of FlashAttention.
- [PyTorch: automatic mixed precision](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/amp.html) — where the fp16 range limit becomes a loss-scaler.
- [PyTorch: numerical accuracy notes](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/numerical_accuracy.html) — the official fine print on reduced-precision reductions, batched-op variance, and why bitwise reproducibility across devices was never promised to you.
